title: 12  Building a Shared Technology and Data Future for the United Nations
crumbs: 12  Building a Shared Technology and Data Future for the United Nations

title: 12  Building a Shared Technology and Data Future for the United Nations
text:
12.3 Key tensions

12.3.1 Shared services versus organisational independence
Some leaders argue that common technology platforms would reduce duplication, improve security, and lower costs.
Others are concerned that centralisation could:

reduce organisational autonomy;
create dependence on a system-wide platform;
fail to meet specialist operational needs;
introduce a single point of failure; or
allow larger entities to dominate decisions.



12.3.2 Open sharing versus responsible restriction
The proposed Data Commons is based on the principle that shareable data should be made available across the UN system.
However, not all information can be treated in the same way. Some data may need to remain restricted because of:

privacy;
humanitarian protection;
national security;
political sensitivity;
agreements with governments or partners;
intellectual-property restrictions; or
risks to vulnerable populations.

The panel must decide how “shareable data” will be defined and who can authorise exceptions.


12.3.3 Common standards versus existing systems
Interoperability requires common definitions, metadata, identifiers, formats, interfaces, and quality expectations.
However, replacing all existing systems would be costly and disruptive. Some entities have already invested heavily in specialist platforms.
The panel must determine whether entities should:

replace their existing systems;
connect them to a shared data backbone;
adopt common standards gradually; or
use a combination of these approaches.



12.3.4 Collective financing versus local budgets
Shared infrastructure creates system-wide benefits, but costs may not be distributed evenly.
Larger entities may be able to contribute more, while smaller entities may benefit significantly but have limited funds.
There is also a risk that entities will continue investing in separate systems while expecting others to finance shared infrastructure.


12.3.5 Voluntary cooperation versus enforceable governance
Previous coordination initiatives have often depended on voluntary participation.
Some stakeholders argue that collaboration will remain limited unless common standards and shared-service requirements are mandatory.
Others argue that formal enforcement would be difficult because participating entities have distinct mandates, governing bodies, and funding sources.
crumbs: 12  Building a Shared Technology and Data Future for the United Nations

title: 12  Building a Shared Technology and Data Future for the United Nations
text:
12.4 Stakeholder roles
Assign one role to each participant. In groups with fewer than five members, participants may combine roles.

12.4.1 Role 1: UN System Transformation Executive
You are a senior leader responsible for delivering organisation-wide reform.

12.4.1.1 Your priorities

Reduce duplication and unnecessary technology spending.
Demonstrate visible progress within nine months.
Build shared capabilities that benefit the entire UN system.
Strengthen the UN’s credibility in a rapidly changing digital environment.
Ensure that reforms support both internal operations and services to Member States.



12.4.1.2 Your concerns

The programme may become delayed by inter-agency negotiations.
Entities may support reform publicly but resist practical changes.
A small pilot may not produce meaningful system-wide savings.
Failure could weaken confidence in future transformation initiatives.



12.4.1.3 Questions you should raise

Which decisions must be made centrally?
What results should be delivered in the first nine months?
How will entities be held accountable for participation?
How will benefits and savings be demonstrated?




12.4.2 Role 2: Chief Information and Technology Officer
You are responsible for the shared technology architecture and the Technology Accelerator Platform.

12.4.2.1 Your priorities

Consolidate duplicative technology services where practical.
Improve cybersecurity, resilience, and technical support.
Develop reusable cloud, connectivity, identity, and integration services.
Avoid replacing specialist systems unnecessarily.
Create a scalable architecture that can support future AI and digital initiatives.



12.4.2.2 Your concerns

Existing systems may be outdated or difficult to integrate.
A single platform could become too complex or vulnerable.
Entities may demand extensive customisation.
Technology may be blamed for problems that are actually caused by weak governance or inconsistent business processes.



12.4.2.3 Questions you should raise

Which services should be shared across the system?
What should remain under local control?
Should the pilot use one common platform or a federated architecture?
What technical standards are essential for interoperability?




12.4.3 Role 3: Chief Data Officer and Data Commons Lead
You are responsible for the governance, interoperability, and long-term value of the UN System Data Commons.

12.4.3.1 Your priorities

Establish common metadata, identifiers, definitions, and interfaces.
Make data easier to find, access, combine, and reuse.
Define clear data ownership and stewardship responsibilities.
Introduce transparent data-quality and lineage information.
Ensure that the Data Commons supports local, regional, and global analysis.



12.4.3.2 Your concerns

The project may create another portal rather than a genuinely connected data system.
Entities may share data without sufficient metadata or quality controls.
Common standards may be treated as optional.
Data may be copied centrally without clear ownership or maintenance responsibilities.



12.4.3.3 Questions you should raise

What does it mean for data to be interoperable?
Who remains accountable for data contributed to the Commons?
What minimum metadata and quality standards should apply?
How should access and reuse decisions be recorded?




12.4.4 Role 4: Humanitarian Protection, Legal, and Ethics Adviser
You are responsible for privacy, protection, legal compliance, security, and ethical use.

12.4.4.1 Your priorities

Prevent data sharing from causing harm to vulnerable people.
Ensure that access is necessary, proportionate, and purpose-specific.
Establish clear restrictions for highly sensitive data.
Require security controls, audit trails, retention rules, and incident procedures.
Ensure that new analytical or AI uses receive appropriate review.



12.4.4.2 Your concerns

“Openly shared” may be interpreted too broadly.
Data collected for humanitarian assistance could later be used for political, immigration, security, or enforcement purposes.
Combining datasets may reveal sensitive information even when individual sources appear low-risk.
Pressure for rapid delivery may weaken protection safeguards.



12.4.4.3 Questions you should raise

Which categories of data must remain separate or restricted?
Who decides whether sharing creates an unacceptable risk?
Can aggregated or de-identified data meet the intended need?
What forms of redress and accountability are required?




12.4.5 Role 5: Representative of a Participating UN Entity or Member State Partner
You represent an organisation that produces valuable data but has limited financial and technical capacity.

12.4.5.1 Your priorities

Preserve the ability to meet your organisation’s specific mandate.
Ensure that shared systems respond to local and operational needs.
Receive financial and technical support for participation.
Avoid unfunded requirements or forced replacement of effective systems.
Ensure that Member States and local partners benefit from the transformation.



12.4.5.2 Your concerns

Larger entities may control the platform and governance process.
Central standards may not reflect local contexts.
Your organisation may contribute data but receive little practical benefit.
Shared infrastructure may create new costs or dependencies.
Data may be interpreted without sufficient country or sector context.



12.4.5.3 Questions you should raise

What incentives and support will participating entities receive?
How will smaller organisations influence decisions?
Who pays for system integration and ongoing maintenance?
How will local context and Member State needs be preserved?

title: 12  Building a Shared Technology and Data Future for the United Nations
text:
12.7 Prioritised discussion questions
1. Should the UN adopt a centralised, federated, or hybrid model for shared technology and data?
Consider cost, interoperability, security, resilience, organisational independence, and the feasibility of implementation across diverse entities.
2. Which decisions should be made at the UN system level, and which should remain with individual entities?
Distinguish responsibilities for standards, infrastructure, data ownership, access, quality, security, and operational use.
3. What five controls must be mandatory before data is integrated into the UN System Data Commons?
Prioritise the controls that are most important for producing useful, secure, and trustworthy information.
4. How should the UN define “shareable data,” and who should have authority to approve restrictions or exceptions?
Consider privacy, humanitarian protection, political sensitivity, public benefit, local context, and the risk of future data misuse.
5. What financing and governance incentives would make organisations genuinely collaborate rather than continue building separate systems?
Consider mandatory standards, shared funding, subsidies, cost recovery, representation in decision-making, technical support, and consequences for unnecessary duplication.

title: 13  Reclaiming Authority over Indigenous Data
text:
13.4 The immediate decision
The Red Willow Tribal Council has created a Data Sovereignty and Governance Negotiation Panel.
Your group is that panel.
You must recommend whether the Nation should:

13.4.1 Option A: Accept the proposed partnership with limited amendments
Proceed quickly and rely mainly on the university and federal agency for technical governance.


13.4.2 Option B: Reject the partnership and build an independent tribal platform
Retain full control, even if implementation takes longer and requires new funding.

13.4.2.1 Option C: Negotiate an Indigenous-led partnership
Proceed only if the agreement recognises the Nation’s sovereign authority and establishes Indigenous-led governance, ownership, capacity development, and purpose-specific access.



13.4.3 Option D: Adopt a phased approach
Begin with a small number of low- or moderate-sensitivity datasets while the Nation develops its governance laws, review mechanisms, workforce, and technical infrastructure.
The group may combine or modify these options, but it must explain its decision.
crumbs: 13  Reclaiming Authority over Indigenous Data

title: 13  Reclaiming Authority over Indigenous Data
text:
13.5 Stakeholder roles
Assign one role to each participant. In groups with fewer than five members, participants may combine roles.

13.5.1 Role 1: Tribal Council Leader
You are an elected leader of Red Willow Nation and chair the negotiation panel.

13.5.1.1 Your priorities

Protect the Nation’s sovereignty and long-term interests.
Ensure that data contribute to nation rebuilding and effective government.
Obtain useful information for urgent policy and funding decisions.
Maintain accountability to citizens, elders, and future generations.
Reach an agreement that the Tribal Council can realistically implement.



13.5.1.2 Your concerns

The Nation may surrender authority in exchange for short-term resources.
Delaying the project may leave leaders without data needed for urgent decisions.
A governance framework may be approved without adequate staff or funding.
External organisations may treat the Nation as a stakeholder rather than a sovereign government.



13.5.1.3 Questions you should raise

Which decisions must remain exclusively with the Nation?
What benefits must the partnership deliver?
What should happen if partners fail to follow tribal requirements?
Is the proposed model sustainable after the grant ends?




13.5.2 Role 2: Tribal Data Governance and Planning Director
You lead the Nation’s data, planning, and administrative improvement work.

13.5.2.1 Your priorities

Build a strong tribal data system.
Establish data ownership, stewardship, quality, metadata, and access rules.
Obtain accurate and relevant data for tribal decision-making.
Reduce long-term dependence on external organisations.
Develop a tribal workforce and technical infrastructure.



13.5.2.2 Your concerns

Tribal departments currently use inconsistent data practices.
External datasets may not match tribal definitions or priorities.
The Nation may lack the capacity to enforce a complex agreement.
Data may be copied into an external platform without clear lineage or correction processes.



13.5.2.3 Questions you should raise

Which data assets are most important for tribal governance?
What minimum data standards should the Nation establish?
Who will be responsible for quality and correction?
What training, infrastructure, and knowledge transfer must be funded?




13.5.3 Role 3: Cultural Knowledge Holder and Community Representative
You represent elders, cultural practitioners, families, and community members whose information and knowledge may be included.

13.5.3.1 Your priorities

Ensure that cultural protocols govern culturally sensitive data.
Protect collective knowledge from inappropriate access or commercial use.
Prevent research from describing the Nation only through problems and deficits.
Require transparency, reciprocity, and meaningful community benefit.
Protect responsibilities to ancestors and future generations.



13.5.3.2 Your concerns

Individual consent may be treated as sufficient for collectively held knowledge.
De-identification may not prevent group or cultural harm.
Sacred knowledge could be made accessible to unauthorised people.
Community consultation may occur after important decisions have already been made.



13.5.3.3 Questions you should raise

Which information should never enter the proposed platform?
Who has cultural authority to approve access?
How should community values shape indicators and interpretation?
How will citizens challenge harmful or inaccurate representations?




13.5.4 Role 4: University Research and Technology Partner
You represent the university offering technical infrastructure and research expertise.

13.5.4.1 Your priorities

Build a technically robust and useful platform.
Produce research that improves health and community wellbeing.
Meet funding, research-integrity, and data-management requirements.
Make appropriate data reusable for future scientific work.
Establish a partnership that can attract continued investment.



13.5.4.2 Your concerns

Highly restrictive rules may make research impractical.
Project delays may jeopardise funding.
Different approval processes for each use may create high administrative costs.
The Nation may expect the university to assume risks without sufficient operational authority.



13.5.4.3 Questions you should raise

What forms of research reuse could be approved in advance?
How will review decisions be made within project timelines?
What technical responsibilities should remain with the university?
How can scientific transparency coexist with tribal control?




13.5.5 Role 5: Federal Agency and Funding Representative
You represent the federal agency providing data and contributing funding.

13.5.5.1 Your priorities

Improve public programmes and health outcomes.
Support comparability with national indicators.
Ensure lawful, secure, and accountable use of federal data.
Demonstrate responsible use of public funding.
Develop a model that may inform future government-to-government partnerships.



13.5.5.2 Your concerns

Tribal and federal definitions may not align.
Agency rules may limit the transfer or localisation of some records.
A highly customised system may be difficult to sustain.
Other federal programmes may expect access to project outputs.




13.5.6 Questions you should raise

How should federal and tribal standards be reconciled?
What nation-to-nation agreement is required?
Which outputs can be reported publicly?
How should future federal requests for access be handled?

13.8 Prioritised discussion questions
1. Who should have final authority over data about Red Willow citizens, lands, resources, and cultures?
Distinguish sovereign authority, ownership, custody, stewardship, technical control, and research responsibility.
2. What five conditions are essential for an external data partnership to support rather than weaken Indigenous data sovereignty?
Prioritise the conditions that most directly affect authority, collective benefit, responsibility, ethics, capacity, and long-term control.
3. How should the Nation decide which data can be shared widely, shared under controlled access, or retained under exclusive tribal control?

4. How can the Nation gain technical expertise, funding, and access to externally held data without creating a new form of data dependency?

title: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda
text:
14.1 Case Overview
AquaVolt Wakanda is the oldest and largest private provider of water and electricity utilities in Wakanda. For decades, the company has played a central role in supporting households, tourism, commerce, and public infrastructure across the islands. Its scale and longevity have made it an essential part of national life, but its operating model has remained heavily dependent on legacy processes.
Recently, the company appointed Chantal, its youngest-ever Director of Innovations, to help lead a modernization agenda. Her mandate is to improve how the company delivers utility services to customers within a 12-month period. She has been asked to focus on practical, visible, and measurable improvements rather than broad transformation rhetoric. The challenge is to modernize a long-established utility without disrupting service continuity, alienating experienced employees, or overextending the company’s investment capacity.
This case places participants in the role of Chantal and her planning team. Their task is to develop a one-year modernization plan that balances operational realism, customer expectations, organizational change, and financial discipline.
crumbs: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda

title: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda
text:
14.2 Organizational Background
AquaVolt Wakanda is a privately owned utility company serving a geographically dispersed market with a mix of urban, suburban, commercial, and tourism-driven demand. The company has an extensive network of water distribution lines, electrical infrastructure, customer service operations, and field maintenance teams. Its assets are critical to economic stability and service continuity.
The company’s reputation has historically been built on reliability, reach, and technical competence. Senior managers and engineers have long relied on experience-based judgement and established operating rules to manage infrastructure and customer service decisions. This has ensured continuity, but it has also created a strong dependence on legacy systems and informal practices.
Although AquaVolt remains operationally important and commercially viable, the company now faces increasing pressure to modernize. Customers expect faster, more accurate, and more transparent service. Internal leadership increasingly recognizes that manual processes are constraining efficiency and limiting the company’s ability to respond to demand growth and service complexity.
crumbs: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda

title: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda
text:
14.3 The Appointment of Chantal
Chantal’s appointment as Director of Innovations is significant for both symbolic and practical reasons. She is younger than most of the company’s senior managers and is seen as someone who can bring new thinking to an organization shaped by tradition. Her credibility comes from her ability to work across functions, challenge outdated assumptions constructively, and translate ideas into implementable action.
Her role is not purely technological. Instead, she is expected to lead cross-functional modernization efforts across operations, customer service, finance, engineering, and IT. She must identify where the company’s current systems create inefficiency, determine which changes will deliver the greatest impact, and build support for change across a workforce with deeply embedded habits.
Chantal reports to executive leadership, but she does not have unilateral authority over major capital decisions or operational redesign. She must therefore influence rather than dictate, making stakeholder alignment a critical part of her success.
crumbs: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda

title: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda
crumbs: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda

title: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda
text:
14.5 Strategic Problem Statement
AquaVolt Wakanda must modernize its service delivery model while preserving the reliability and trust that have defined the company for decades. The core strategic problem is not simply whether to adopt new technology, but how to redesign core utility operations in a way that improves billing accuracy, infrastructure planning, customer experience, and internal efficiency within a single year.
Chantal’s challenge is to identify a transformation pathway that is credible, affordable, operationally safe, and politically acceptable within the company.
crumbs: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda

title: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda
text:
14.6 Business Pressures
The company is facing a convergence of internal and external pressures.
From the customer perspective, there is growing frustration with estimated bills, service delays, and the lack of transparent communication. Customers increasingly compare utility experiences to digital-first services in banking, telecommunications, and retail, where accuracy and responsiveness are expected as standard.
From an operational perspective, manual meter reading and outdated planning rules reduce productivity and complicate decision-making. Errors must be corrected, disputes resolved, and field teams rechecked, all of which increase cost and strain staff capacity.
From a leadership perspective, the company must improve performance without destabilizing core operations. Executives recognize the need for modernization, but they are cautious about large-scale disruption, particularly in a utility context where service continuity is non-negotiable.
From a financial perspective, AquaVolt must justify any investment through tangible gains such as reduced rework, lower operating costs, better billing accuracy, improved revenue assurance, or more efficient use of field resources.
crumbs: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda

title: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda
text:
14.7 Constraints on the Transformation
Chantal’s 12-month modernization agenda is shaped by several important constraints.
First, the company cannot replace all legacy systems at once. Existing billing and operational systems may not integrate easily with modern tools, making a full-scale overhaul too risky.
Second, the utility environment requires continuous service delivery. Any modernization effort must be phased carefully so that it does not interrupt meter reading, billing cycles, maintenance operations, or customer support.
Third, the organization has a strong legacy culture. Experienced staff may view new digital processes as unnecessary, intrusive, or unrealistic. Without strong change management, even a technically sound plan may fail in practice.
Fourth, the company operates in a geographically complex environment. Field access, transportation, scheduling, and service routing are all influenced by island logistics and local terrain.
Finally, the company’s modernization budget is limited. Chantal must therefore prioritize initiatives that can demonstrate early value and create a foundation for later expansion.
crumbs: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda

title: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda
text:
14.8 Stakeholder Landscape
A successful transformation will require cooperation from multiple stakeholder groups.
Executive leadership wants improved performance, visible progress, and a credible strategic narrative.
Operations managers want practical solutions that improve field efficiency and service reliability.
Finance leaders want measurable returns and disciplined capital allocation.
Customer service teams want fewer complaints, fewer corrections, and better visibility into account issues.
Field technicians and meter readers need tools and processes that make their work simpler rather than more burdensome.
IT staff must support integration, data quality, and system stability.
Regulators and public authorities expect fair service standards, accurate billing, and dependable utility performance.
Customers want timely service, transparent bills, and faster resolution of issues.
crumbs: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda

title: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda
text:
14.9 Chantal’s Vision
Chantal’s vision is to modernize AquaVolt Wakanda in a way that makes the company more responsive, more efficient, and more customer-centred. She is not seeking transformation for its own sake. Instead, she wants the company to become easier to run and easier to trust.
She believes the organization can achieve meaningful progress in three areas over 12 months:

Digitize and improve meter reading processes so that billing becomes more accurate, timely, and transparent.
Redesign field planning and resource allocation using more data-informed criteria rather than outdated rules.
Improve operational visibility by providing management with better reporting, monitoring, and decision-support tools.

Her objective is to create a modernization model that can be scaled later, but that first proves itself through practical results.
crumbs: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda

title: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda
crumbs: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda

title: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda
text:
14.11 Discussion Questions

What should Chantal prioritize in the first 90 days?
Which modernization initiative is likely to create the fastest visible value?
How can the company reduce resistance from field teams and supervisors?
What data and systems are required to replace archaic resource-positioning rules?
How can AquaVolt improve service quality without disrupting billing continuity?
What metrics should be used to assess success at the end of 12 months?
How should Chantal balance short-term operational improvements with longer-term transformation goals?
crumbs: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda

title: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda
text:
14.12 Teaching Note
This case is designed to test participants’ ability to develop an actionable transformation roadmap in a legacy utility setting. It emphasizes operational realism, stakeholder management, phased implementation, and the tension between innovation and continuity.
Participants should avoid proposing a purely technology-led solution. Instead, they should treat the problem as a combined process, people, data, and governance challenge. The strongest responses will align modernization with customer value, internal efficiency, and organizational readiness.
crumbs: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda

title: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda
text:
14.13 Conclusion
AquaVolt Wakanda is at an important inflection point. Its legacy operating model has supported decades of service delivery, but the company now faces growing pressure to modernize. Chantal’s appointment reflects the organization’s recognition that the future will require different tools, different rules, and different ways of working.
The central question is whether AquaVolt can modernize quickly enough to matter, carefully enough to avoid disruption, and credibly enough to build trust with customers and employees alike. The planning team’s task is to turn that strategic question into a practical one-year roadmap.
crumbs: 14  Modernising Utility Operations at AquaVolt Wakanda

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
text:
15.1 Case Overview
Jean Paul is a mid-career researcher and academic at Caledonia University, a prestigious institution with a strong reputation for interdisciplinary research across public health, environmental science, data analytics, and policy studies. He works within the university’s Department of Health and Environment, where several research units examine the relationships between environmental exposures, population health, urban systems, and social wellbeing.
Over the past five years, Jean Paul and a small team of researchers have built a valuable environmental evidence base using air quality logs collected from a network of sensors deployed throughout the surrounding metropolitan area. Their research has been strengthened by additional area-based datasets on vehicular traffic, land use, and the locations of factories and other industrial plants. This work has enabled the team to identify patterns in air pollution levels, seasonal variation, hotspot locations, and likely determinants of poor air quality.
Jean Paul now wants to take the research further. He believes the next major question is not only where poor air quality occurs and what drives it, but also what it is doing to people’s health. To answer that question, he wants to link the existing air quality dataset with health and health outcome data held by his departmental colleagues. His ambition is to move from descriptive environmental analysis toward integrated, evidence-based research that can support public health understanding, policy engagement, and future intervention design.
The case places participants in the role of Jean Paul and his wider research team. Their task is to develop a research strategy that will allow the group to expand from air quality analysis into cross-disciplinary work on air quality and health outcomes while dealing with the practical, ethical, and methodological challenges involved in combining environmental and health data.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
text:
15.2 Suggested Research Group Name
Jean Paul’s research group is called the AeroHealth Evidence Lab (AHEL).
The name reflects the group’s focus on: - Aero: air quality, pollution, and environmental exposure; - Health: health impacts and outcomes; - Evidence: rigorous, data-driven analysis; - Lab: collaborative, interdisciplinary research design.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
text:
15.3 Organizational Background
Caledonia University is a large, research-intensive institution with multiple departments and affiliated research centres. Within the Department of Health and Environment, the culture is highly collaborative but methodologically diverse. Some teams focus on epidemiology and population health, others on environmental monitoring, geospatial analysis, urban systems, or policy evaluation.
The department has a history of producing respected research, securing external grants, and contributing to public debate. However, like many universities, it also operates within separate data, ethics, and disciplinary silos. Research units often manage their own datasets, technical approaches, and publication priorities. As a result, promising opportunities for integration can be slowed by different data standards, different analytical methods, and different expectations around ownership and collaboration.
Jean Paul’s team has already demonstrated that it can generate valuable environmental intelligence. The next stage of the work requires close partnership with colleagues who hold or manage health-related data. That shift introduces both opportunity and complexity.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
text:
15.4 Jean Paul’s Role
Jean Paul is an experienced academic who has built his career around the intersection of environmental science and data-driven policy relevance. He is respected for his technical competence, his ability to work across disciplines, and his skill in turning raw data into policy-relevant insight.
He leads a small team of researchers who have spent the past five years building and refining the air quality project. The team includes staff with skills in sensor data management, statistical analysis, geospatial mapping, and field coordination. Their work has established a strong foundation, but Jean Paul believes the project’s real value will be unlocked only when it is linked to health evidence.
His vision is to evolve the current work into a broader research program that can: - connect environmental exposure to health outcomes, - identify populations most at risk, - produce stronger evidence for policy and prevention, - and position the university as a leader in translational environmental health research.
Jean Paul is aware that this shift will not happen through technical analysis alone. It will require collaboration, data governance, ethics approval, stakeholder alignment, and a carefully designed research roadmap.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
text:
15.5 Current Research Foundation

15.5.1 Air Quality Monitoring Network
Jean Paul’s team has spent five years collecting air quality logs through a distributed network of sensors placed at strategic locations throughout the study area. The network captures temporal and spatial variation in pollution levels across neighbourhoods, road corridors, industrial zones, and mixed-use areas.
The sensor network has allowed the team to build a substantial environmental dataset with information such as: - pollutant concentration trends over time; - hourly, daily, and seasonal changes in air quality; - geographic hotspots and low-exposure zones; - and correlations between pollution levels and nearby activity patterns.
This data has become a core research asset. It provides a detailed picture of environmental exposure and allows the team to move beyond anecdotal observations.


15.5.2 Area-Based Determinants of Air Quality
In addition to sensor logs, the team has collected contextual data on: - vehicular traffic intensity, - road density and traffic corridors, - factory and industrial plant locations, - land-use patterns, - and area characteristics that may influence pollution levels.
This has enabled the team to identify likely determinants of poor air quality and to describe the structural and spatial factors associated with pollution variation. The research has been useful, but largely environmental in scope. It explains where pollution may be concentrated and why, but not yet what those exposure patterns mean for health.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
text:
15.6 Strategic Research Opportunity
Jean Paul believes the next phase of research should examine the relationship between poor air quality and poor health outcomes. He sees an opportunity to create an integrated dataset that combines environmental exposure measures with health data held by colleagues in the department or affiliated research groups.
This would allow the research unit to explore questions such as: - Are areas with persistently poor air quality also associated with worse health outcomes? - Which population groups are most affected? - Are there seasonal or lagged relationships between pollution episodes and health events? - Do exposure patterns differ by neighbourhood, age group, or socioeconomic context? - Can the research identify thresholds or clusters of risk that are useful for policy or intervention design?
Jean Paul’s ambition is to move the research team from environmental observation to health-relevant evidence generation. He wants the group to produce work that is scientifically rigorous, policy-relevant, and capable of supporting public health action.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
text:
15.7 Strategic Problem Statement
The challenge is to develop a research strategy that enables the AeroHealth Evidence Lab to combine air quality and health datasets in a way that is methodologically sound, ethically approved, and operationally feasible. The key strategic question is how to design a research program that uses existing environmental evidence as a foundation for cross-disciplinary health analysis without compromising data quality, privacy, or scientific integrity.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
section: 15.8 Why This Is an Important Next Step
text:
15.8 Why This Is an Important Next Step
The current research has already answered useful questions about pollution patterns and environmental determinants. However, the policy value of air quality research increases significantly when it can be linked to outcomes that matter directly to people’s lives.
By connecting exposure data with health outcomes, Jean Paul’s team could: - strengthen the case for environmental intervention; - identify vulnerable populations or locations; - contribute to hospital, public health, or municipal planning; - produce more publishable and impactful interdisciplinary research; - and help translate academic findings into real-world action.
In short, the team would shift from describing a problem to demonstrating its consequences.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
text:
15.9 Research and Operational Challenges

15.9.1 Data Integration
The first major challenge is combining datasets that were originally collected for different purposes. Air quality logs, traffic data, industrial location data, and health records may differ in structure, scale, time period, geographic resolution, and quality. The research team will need to determine whether the datasets can be aligned meaningfully and what common identifiers or geospatial frameworks can support linkage.


15.9.2 Data Governance and Access
Health data is likely to be sensitive and subject to strict access requirements. Even within a university, the team will need to establish who can access which data, under what permissions, and for what purpose. Ethical approval, confidentiality protections, and institutional data-sharing agreements will be essential.


15.9.3 Methodological Fit
Environmental exposure data and health outcome data often require careful methodological design. The team will need to decide whether the research will be ecological, geospatial, longitudinal, case-control, or some hybrid model. It will also need to address confounders, bias, temporal lag effects, and the challenge of inferring association versus causation.


15.9.4 Interdisciplinary Collaboration
The research is inherently cross-disciplinary. It will require collaboration between environmental scientists, epidemiologists, public health researchers, statisticians, geospatial analysts, and possibly data governance experts. Each discipline may have its own language, priorities, and standards of evidence.


15.9.5 Resource and Time Constraints
Jean Paul’s team is small. That means the project must be realistic in scope and sequenced carefully. A strong research plan will need to identify what can be done immediately, what requires formal approval or partnership, and what may be better suited to a longer-term program.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
text:
15.10 Opportunities for Expansion
Jean Paul sees three major opportunities for the research unit.

15.10.1 1. Build an integrated exposure-health research platform
The team could establish a structured research environment that brings together air quality data, geospatial exposure variables, and health outcome datasets in a repeatable analytical framework. This would support not only one study, but a pipeline of future studies.


15.10.2 2. Generate evidence for targeted public health action
By identifying where and when poor air quality overlaps with poor health outcomes, the research could inform targeted interventions, local public health responses, or community awareness initiatives.


15.10.3 3. Position the unit as an interdisciplinary leader
A successful program would strengthen the profile of the AeroHealth Evidence Lab and the department more broadly. It could support grant applications, publications, conference presentations, and partnerships with external agencies.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
text:
15.11 Operating Constraints
Any research plan must account for the realities of the university environment.
The first constraint is ethical and legal compliance. Health data must be handled with care, and any linkage must respect consent, confidentiality, and institutional requirements.
The second constraint is data compatibility. Datasets may not use the same spatial boundaries, time intervals, or coding conventions.
The third constraint is team capacity. Jean Paul’s team is small, so the project must not depend on overly ambitious manual data cleaning or complex custom infrastructure unless support is available.
The fourth constraint is disciplinary alignment. Colleagues in the health unit may have different research priorities, timelines, or publication expectations.
The fifth constraint is research credibility. Because the project may inform public interest or policy debates, the design must be robust enough to withstand scrutiny from peer reviewers, institutional review boards, and external stakeholders.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
text:
15.12 Stakeholder Landscape
A successful research strategy will require cooperation from several stakeholder groups.
Jean Paul and the AeroHealth Evidence Lab want to expand the research program and increase its relevance and impact.
Departmental colleagues in health research hold valuable outcome data and methodological expertise.
University ethics and governance bodies need assurance that the project handles sensitive data appropriately.
Data or IT support teams may need to assist with storage, access control, and secure analysis environments.
External research partners or public agencies may become important later if the work expands into policy engagement or applied health analysis.
Funding bodies are likely to favour projects that demonstrate interdisciplinary value, methodological rigour, and social relevance.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
text:
15.13 Jean Paul’s Vision
Jean Paul wants the AeroHealth Evidence Lab to evolve into a stronger interdisciplinary research unit that can connect environmental exposure to health outcomes in a meaningful way.
His vision includes: 1. A robust linked dataset combining air quality, environmental determinants, and health outcomes. 2. A clear research agenda focused on identifying associations, patterns, and at-risk populations. 3. A collaborative model that draws on the strengths of health and environment researchers across the department. 4. A stronger external profile through publications, presentations, and research partnerships. 5. Evidence that can support policy and public health action in the future.
He is not looking for a one-off study. He wants to build a durable research capability.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
section: 15.14 Expectations for the Research Planning Exercise
text:
15.14 Expectations for the Research Planning Exercise
Participants should assume that Jean Paul has a solid environmental data foundation but now needs a realistic plan for expanding into health-linked research. The task is not simply to merge datasets. It is to design a credible research strategy that accounts for data access, ethics, methods, collaboration, and dissemination.
A strong research plan is likely to address: - the specific research questions to be pursued; - which health datasets are relevant and how they will be accessed; - how environmental and health data will be linked; - the analytical approach and study design; - ethical and governance requirements; - collaboration roles across departments; - the timeline and sequencing of work; - and the outputs expected from the project.
The plan should also define how success will be measured, such as whether the team can secure data access, complete a pilot linkage, produce a publishable analysis, or establish a scalable research platform.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
text:
15.15 Discussion Questions

What should Jean Paul prioritize in the first 90 days?
Which health outcomes would be the most appropriate starting point for linked analysis?
What data governance and ethics issues are likely to arise?
How can the team ensure the environmental and health datasets are compatible?
What research design would best support the team’s objectives?
How can Jean Paul build collaboration with departmental colleagues who hold health data?
What would success look like after 12 months?
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
text:
15.16 Teaching Note
This case is designed to test participants’ ability to develop a cross-disciplinary research strategy in a university setting. It emphasizes collaboration, ethics, data linkage, and the shift from environmental description to health-oriented evidence generation.
Participants should avoid treating the problem as purely technical. The strongest responses will address research design, institutional partnerships, governance, and the practical realities of academic work.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University
text:
15.17 Conclusion
Jean Paul and the AeroHealth Evidence Lab have already built an important foundation through five years of air quality research. The next step is to connect that evidence to health outcomes so that the work becomes more powerful, more relevant, and more impactful.
The challenge is to create a research strategy that enables this transition while respecting the complexity of health data, the constraints of academic collaboration, and the standards required for credible interdisciplinary research. The task for the planning team is to turn that ambition into a practical and defensible research roadmap.
crumbs: 15  Linking Air Quality and Health Outcomes at Northbridge University

title: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit
text:
16.1 Case Overview
Marie has spent 30 years serving as a government social worker and now leads the Poverty Alleviation Unit as its Director. Over the course of her career, she has witnessed a steady rise in the number of families seeking welfare support. The demand on the unit has grown more complex over time, with more households presenting multiple and overlapping needs, including food insecurity, unemployment, unstable housing, health challenges, and school-related vulnerability.
The unit has built a strong institutional memory and maintains complete records of the families it has supported over the years. However, these records exist only on paper. Case files are stored in physical cabinets, archived in boxes, and accessed manually by staff who often rely on memory, file labels, and experience to retrieve information. This has allowed the unit to function, but it has also limited the ability of leadership to analyze trends, assess demand patterns, and proactively plan services.
Marie believes the unit now needs to move beyond reactive paper-based case work and begin using its data more strategically. She wants to better understand the families it serves, identify patterns in need, and improve the way welfare support is targeted and delivered. At the same time, she recognizes that her staff are largely traditional in their working methods and many are older professionals who have spent their careers managing cases on paper. They are experienced, committed, and deeply knowledgeable, but they may be hesitant to shift to digital systems and new ways of working.
This case asks participants to develop a strategy that will help Marie transform the Poverty Alleviation Unit into a more insight-driven, efficient, and client-centred organization while respecting the experience and concerns of its workforce.
crumbs: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit

title: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit
text:
16.2 Organizational Background
The Poverty Alleviation Unit is a public-sector service unit responsible for identifying vulnerable households, assessing social and economic need, coordinating welfare assistance, and maintaining records of support interventions. Its work is highly people-centred and often sensitive, involving confidential personal information and complex family circumstances. Staff members may conduct home visits, receive walk-in requests, coordinate referrals, review eligibility, and track follow-up actions over time.
The unit has historically operated through a paper-based workflow. Case notes, application forms, assessment reports, referral letters, follow-up records, and approval documents are all maintained in physical files. This has created a detailed archive of family histories, but one that is difficult to search, analyze, and update at scale.
The unit’s long-standing practices have helped preserve continuity, especially in an environment where trust, discretion, and personal relationships matter. Many staff members have developed strong expertise through years of direct case work. However, as demand has increased, these traditional methods are becoming harder to sustain. The unit now faces pressure to improve efficiency, strengthen reporting, and make better use of the information it already holds.
crumbs: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit

title: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit
text:
16.3 Marie’s Role
Marie is a respected public servant who has worked in the Poverty Alleviation Unit for three decades. She began her career as a frontline social worker and has gradually taken on more responsibility, developing a deep understanding of the families served by the unit as well as the operational realities of public welfare administration.
As Director, Marie is now responsible for setting the tone for modernization. She is not driven by technology for its own sake. Instead, she wants to create a system that helps the unit serve families more effectively, identify needs earlier, and make better decisions about resource allocation and intervention design.
Marie’s credibility comes from experience, empathy, and familiarity with the realities of field work. She understands that transformation will only succeed if it is practical, respectful of existing expertise, and aligned with the mission of the unit. She is aware that introducing digital case work will require more than new software. It will require changes in process, behaviour, training, governance, and leadership.
crumbs: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit

title: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit
text:
16.4 Current Operating Challenges

16.4.1 Paper-Based Case Records
The unit holds a rich archive of family records, but the information is dispersed across folders, filing cabinets, and storage rooms. Staff must manually search for files, compare handwritten notes, and piece together client histories from multiple documents. This makes it difficult to respond quickly to inquiries or review past support arrangements.
The paper-based system also creates several operational problems:

retrieving information is time-consuming;
files can be incomplete, misfiled, or damaged;
duplicate records may exist for the same family;
trends are difficult to identify without manual review;
and data sharing across teams is limited.

Although the paper records contain valuable information, they are underused as a strategic asset.


16.4.2 Rising Demand and Case Complexity
Marie has observed a steady increase in the number of families requiring support. This may reflect broader economic pressures, changes in household structure, rising living costs, or a greater awareness of available welfare services. Whatever the cause, the result is clear: more families are coming to the unit, and many are presenting more complicated and long-term needs than before.
The unit’s staff now face greater caseload pressure, more follow-ups, and more coordination with external partners such as schools, health services, and community organizations. As demand rises, the limitations of paper systems become more visible. Staff spend more time searching for information and less time analysing need or planning interventions.


16.4.3 Traditional Staff Culture
The unit’s workforce is experienced and service-oriented, but many staff members are comfortable with the paper methods they have used for years. Their reluctance to shift to digital case work is not necessarily resistance to improvement. In many cases, it reflects legitimate concerns:

fear of losing familiar routines,
concern about the learning curve,
worry that digital systems may slow them down,
uncertainty about confidentiality and data security,
and scepticism that new tools will actually help frontline work.

This cultural factor is one of Marie’s biggest challenges. A technology rollout that ignores staff sentiment may fail even if the tools themselves are sound.
crumbs: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit

title: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit
text:
16.5 Strategic Problem Statement
The Poverty Alleviation Unit has a valuable but underutilized archive of client information. The challenge is to transform that paper-based record system into a more useful source of insight and operational decision-making, while maintaining trust, protecting confidentiality, and supporting staff through the transition to digital case management.
Marie’s strategic question is how to modernize case work in a way that improves service quality and organizational intelligence without undermining staff confidence or the unit’s client-centered culture.
crumbs: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit

title: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit
section: 16.6 Why the Current Model Is Becoming Unsustainable
text:
16.6 Why the Current Model Is Becoming Unsustainable
The current operating model has several long-term risks.
First, it limits the unit’s ability to understand who is using services, how needs are changing, and what types of interventions are most effective. Without structured data, decision-making relies too heavily on anecdote and memory.
Second, it makes planning difficult. Leadership cannot easily estimate demand by geography, household type, age group, referral source, or severity of need. That weakens the unit’s ability to advocate for resources or design targeted programs.
Third, it creates administrative inefficiency. Staff time is consumed by filing, searching, filing again, and reconstructing information rather than focusing on assessment and support.
Fourth, it exposes the unit to continuity risks. Paper files are vulnerable to physical damage, loss, duplication, and inconsistent storage practices.
Finally, it constrains the unit’s ability to collaborate with other agencies. If the unit cannot easily summarize caseloads or produce timely insights, it becomes harder to demonstrate impact or coordinate with partners.
crumbs: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit

title: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit
text:
16.7 Opportunities for Improvement
Marie sees three major opportunities.

16.7.1 1. Create a usable digital record of client history
The unit can begin capturing case information in a structured digital format, either by converting paper records into a searchable database or by starting with new cases and gradually digitizing historical files. This would allow staff to search records more efficiently, retrieve family histories faster, and reduce duplication.


16.7.2 2. Use data to understand client needs more deeply
Once records are digitized and structured, the unit could analyze patterns such as repeat support requests, common referral pathways, geographic concentrations of need, seasonal demand changes, and the types of assistance most frequently required. This would help Marie understand the client population more clearly and design better interventions.


16.7.3 3. Improve service coordination and planning
With better information, the unit could identify service gaps earlier, prioritize high-need households more effectively, and strengthen coordination with external agencies. Over time, the data could help leadership move from reactive case handling to more preventive and targeted support.
crumbs: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit

title: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit
text:
16.8 Operating Constraints
Any strategy must account for the realities of the unit’s environment.
The first constraint is confidentiality. Social welfare records contain highly sensitive personal and family information. Any digital solution must protect privacy, control access carefully, and comply with public-sector record keeping standards.
The second constraint is staff readiness. A significant portion of the team may not be comfortable with digital tools or may need substantial support to change established habits.
The third constraint is resource availability. Public-sector budgets are often limited, which means Marie may need to phase implementation and make a strong case for investment.
The fourth constraint is data quality. Paper files may contain inconsistencies, missing fields, or handwritten notes that are difficult to standardize. A digitization process will need clear rules for classification and data cleaning.
The fifth constraint is continuity of service. Families cannot be disrupted while the unit transitions to a new system. Case work must continue throughout the change process.
crumbs: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit

title: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit
text:
16.9 Stakeholder Landscape
A successful strategy will require support from several stakeholder groups.
Marie and senior leadership want better visibility into caseloads, stronger planning tools, and a more responsive service model.
Frontline social workers want a system that helps them manage cases efficiently without undermining the personal nature of their work.
Administrative staff need simple, reliable processes for file management, data entry, and retrieval.
IT or digital support teams may need to assist with system design, security, and implementation.
Government decision-makers will likely want evidence that the modernization effort improves efficiency, accountability, and outcomes.
Partner agencies such as schools, health providers, and community organizations may benefit from better coordination and clearer referral processes.
Client families need a service that remains respectful, confidential, and accessible during and after the transition.
crumbs: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit

title: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit
text:
16.10 Marie’s Vision
Marie wants the Poverty Alleviation Unit to become more than a repository of paper files. She wants it to become a learning organization that uses its own history to better understand the present and plan for the future.
Her vision includes:

A digitized and searchable case record system that reduces time spent retrieving files and improves continuity of care.
A better understanding of client needs and trends through structured data and reporting.
A workforce that remains experienced and human-centred, but is supported by modern tools that make case work easier and more effective.
Stronger planning and targeting of welfare support, based on real evidence rather than only historical habit or intuition.

Marie does not want technology to replace judgement, empathy, or professional discretion. She wants it to strengthen them.
crumbs: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit

title: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit
crumbs: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit

title: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit
text:
16.12 Discussion Questions

What should Marie prioritize in the first 90 days?
Should the unit digitize all historical records, or begin with new cases first?
How can Marie reduce resistance among older staff members?
What types of client data would be most valuable to capture in a digital system?
How can confidentiality and accessibility be balanced in a digital case work environment?
What operational or social indicators should the unit use to understand client needs better?
How can the unit use its records to improve planning without becoming overly bureaucratic?
crumbs: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit

title: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit
text:
16.13 Teaching Note
This case is designed to test participants’ ability to design a practical digital and organizational transition in a public-service setting. It highlights the tension between tradition and modernization, the importance of staff trust, and the value of turning existing records into decision-support assets.
Participants should avoid framing this as a pure IT implementation. The stronger approach is to treat it as a service redesign challenge involving people, process, data, governance, and change management.
crumbs: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit

title: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit
text:
16.14 Conclusion
The Poverty Alleviation Unit has years of accumulated experience and a complete historical record of the families it has supported. Yet most of that knowledge remains locked in paper files. Marie’s leadership presents an opportunity to convert that legacy into a more usable, insightful, and strategic asset.
The challenge is to modernize in a way that strengthens, rather than replaces, the values at the heart of the unit: compassion, confidentiality, dignity, and practical support for families in need. The strategy team’s task is to turn that vision into a realistic path forward.
crumbs: 16  Modernising Case Work in the Poverty Alleviation Unit

title: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance
text:
17.1 Case Overview
Alain leads Resilient Futures Alliance (RFA), a non-governmental organisation that supports families affected by natural disasters. The organisation works across flood, cyclone, landslide, and storm-response settings, providing rapid humanitarian assistance, family tracing support, emergency referrals, temporary shelter coordination, and recovery follow-up. Over time, RFA has become known for its disciplined field operations and its ability to reach affected households quickly after an event.
A defining feature of RFA’s response model is the quality of its data. In every disaster it responds to, field workers use a phone-based data collection app to capture detailed information about each individual and family supported. The app is deployed on mobile devices carried by response staff and allows the organisation to record household composition, location, emergency needs, food and shelter assistance delivered, vulnerability markers, referral status, and follow-up notes. This has created a highly structured and reliable record of RFA’s work across multiple disaster events.
Alain now believes the organisation has reached an important turning point. While the data is already valuable for internal accountability and operational planning, he sees much greater potential if it can be shared with relevant government departments. His vision is for RFA’s disaster response data to support broader analytics across the public sector, helping authorities better understand who is affected, where needs cluster, which interventions are most effective, and how future disaster responses can be improved.
The case places participants in the role of Alain and his leadership team. Their task is to develop a plan that enables secure, responsible, and practical data sharing and collaboration with government agencies while preserving trust, protecting vulnerable people, and maintaining the speed and flexibility required in disaster response.
crumbs: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance

title: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance
text:
17.2 Suggested Organisation Name
The organisation is called Resilient Futures Alliance (RFA).
The name reflects: - Resilient: the ability of communities and systems to recover after shocks; - Futures: a focus on preparedness, recovery, and long-term learning; - Alliance: the partnership-oriented nature of the NGO’s work with communities, donors, and public-sector agencies.
crumbs: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance

title: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance
text:
17.3 Organisational Background
Resilient Futures Alliance is a disaster-response NGO that works alongside communities, local authorities, and humanitarian partners during and after emergencies. Its mandate is centred on supporting families who have been displaced, injured, economically affected, or otherwise made vulnerable by natural disasters.
RFA’s operations are built around rapid deployment, structured field assessment, and coordinated case management. The organisation has invested in a digital-first approach to field data collection, using a mobile app that allows workers to enter information directly at the point of contact. This has reduced transcription error, improved consistency, and allowed the organisation to track support activities across multiple locations and response phases.
Over several years, this approach has generated a rich archive of operational and household-level data. The organisation now has the ability to review patterns across disasters, understand the scale of assistance delivered, and examine recurring needs among affected populations. However, most of this insight remains within the NGO itself.
Alain believes RFA’s data could have much greater public value if it were made available in a controlled and responsible way to the government departments responsible for disaster management, social protection, housing, public health, and recovery planning.
crumbs: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance

title: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance
text:
17.4 Alain’s Role
Alain is the founder and executive director of RFA. He has spent many years working in humanitarian response and has developed a strong reputation for operational discipline, community trust, and pragmatic leadership. He is comfortable with field realities and understands that the true measure of disaster response is not only how much aid is distributed, but how quickly people’s lives stabilize afterwards.
His current focus is not simply on collecting data, but on converting operational data into a shared public good. He sees several possible benefits: - better coordination with government during active response; - stronger evidence for planning future disaster interventions; - improved identification of vulnerable households; - reduced duplication across service providers; - and more informed policy discussions about preparedness and recovery.
Alain is aware, however, that data sharing with government is not straightforward. It requires legal clarity, technical interoperability, trust, and governance arrangements that respect the rights of affected families.
crumbs: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance

title: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance
text:
17.5 Current Data Collection Practice
RFA’s field workers use a mobile phone-based app to collect data in the field during disaster response and recovery operations. The app is designed for use in fast-moving environments where connectivity may be unreliable and time with each household may be limited.
The dataset typically includes: - household identifier information; - names and demographic details of individuals supported; - disaster type and location; - immediate needs assessed at the time of contact; - assistance provided by RFA; - referral outcomes; - case status and follow-up notes; - and time-stamped fieldworker records.
Because the app is used consistently across response teams, the data is comparatively structured and complete. This gives RFA a strong evidence base for internal monitoring and accountability. It also means the organisation is better positioned than many NGOs to participate in multi-agency analytics or coordinated response planning.
The challenge is that the data was originally designed for service delivery and case tracking, not necessarily for cross-government integration. Alain therefore needs a strategy that preserves the integrity of the original collection process while making the data usable for broader institutional analysis.
crumbs: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance

title: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance
text:
17.6 Strategic Opportunity
Alain’s long-term vision is to create a formal mechanism through which RFA’s disaster response data can be shared with the relevant government departments in a way that supports analytics, planning, and decision-making. He believes this could help government agencies answer questions such as: - Which geographic areas are most consistently affected by particular disaster types? - Which household groups are most vulnerable before and after an event? - What kinds of assistance are delivered most often, and where are gaps emerging? - How quickly are affected families receiving support across different agencies? - What patterns in response data can inform preparedness and resource allocation?
He sees this as an opportunity to shift from fragmented response activity to a more coordinated, data-informed ecosystem for disaster management and recovery.
crumbs: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance

title: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance
text:
17.7 Strategic Problem Statement
The central problem is how to design a secure, ethical, and practical model for sharing disaster response data between a non-governmental organisation and government departments so that the data can be used for analytics and future planning without compromising confidentiality, consent, trust, or operational effectiveness.
Alain’s challenge is to determine what should be shared, with whom, under what conditions, in what format, and for what purposes.
crumbs: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance

title: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance
text:
17.8 Why This Matters
RFA’s data is potentially valuable far beyond the organisation itself. Disaster events often create overlapping needs across humanitarian, health, infrastructure, housing, and social protection systems. If data remains siloed within individual actors, the broader system may miss important patterns or duplicate effort.
By enabling collaboration with government, RFA could help: - improve the targeting of aid; - support faster and more coordinated recovery; - identify high-risk households and communities earlier; - strengthen disaster preparedness using historical evidence; - and create a more complete view of the needs of disaster-affected populations.
The potential value is not only operational. It is also strategic and institutional. A successful data-sharing model could position RFA as a trusted partner in a wider public-data ecosystem.
crumbs: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance

title: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance
text:
17.9 Key Challenges

17.9.1 Data Governance and Confidentiality
RFA’s records contain sensitive personal and household-level information. Disaster-affected families may have experienced loss, displacement, injury, or trauma. Any sharing arrangement must protect privacy, define data access carefully, and ensure that information is used only for legitimate purposes.


17.9.2 Consent and Trust
Affected families may be willing to share information with RFA because they trust its field workers, but that trust may not automatically extend to government agencies. Alain must consider how consent is obtained, how it is communicated, and whether data sharing is consistent with the expectations set at the point of collection.


17.9.3 Data Standardisation and Interoperability
Government departments may use different formats, classification systems, or geographic coding conventions. RFA’s app data may need to be cleaned, mapped, or transformed before it can be integrated into analytics systems used by public agencies.


17.9.4 Institutional Alignment
Different government departments may have different priorities and data maturity levels. Disaster management teams may want real-time information, while social protection teams may be focused on eligibility and follow-up. Health or housing agencies may need different subsets of data. Alain will need a flexible model that can serve multiple stakeholders without creating confusion.


17.9.5 Operational Continuity
RFA’s primary purpose is field response. Any data-sharing arrangement must not slow down frontline work or create extra burden for field workers during an emergency.


17.9.6 Security and Risk
The organisation must be confident that shared data will be protected against misuse, unauthorized access, or re-identification. It also needs a plan for auditability and accountability in case of data incidents.
crumbs: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance

title: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance
text:
17.10 Stakeholder Landscape
A successful collaboration will require cooperation from multiple stakeholder groups.
Alain and the RFA leadership team want data to be useful beyond internal reporting and to support a broader public good.
Field workers need a system that remains fast and practical in high-pressure disaster environments.
Affected families need assurance that their personal information is handled respectfully and securely.
Government disaster management departments want timely, reliable data that improves coordination and planning.
Social protection, health, housing, and recovery agencies may use the data for targeted interventions and post-disaster support.
Technical teams and data governance officials will likely need to design secure exchange mechanisms, access controls, and integration standards.
Donors and partner organisations may support the effort if it strengthens transparency, accountability, and impact.
crumbs: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance

title: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance
text:
17.11 Alain’s Vision
Alain wants RFA to become more than a response organisation. He wants it to contribute to a data-informed disaster ecosystem in which NGOs and government can work from a shared evidence base.
His vision includes: 1. Structured and shareable disaster response data that can be safely used by government departments. 2. A formal data-sharing arrangement that clarifies roles, permissions, and responsibilities. 3. Improved analytics for future disaster preparedness and recovery based on combined datasets. 4. Stronger collaboration between humanitarian and public-sector actors during and after disaster events. 5. A model that preserves trust and operational speed while increasing the public value of the data collected.
Alain is not seeking to relinquish control of the organisation’s data for its own sake. He wants to ensure the data contributes to better outcomes for communities affected by disasters.
crumbs: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance

title: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance
text:
17.12 Opportunities for Expansion
Alain sees three major opportunities.

17.12.1 1. Establish a government-facing data-sharing framework
RFA could develop a policy and technical framework that defines which data elements can be shared, in what form, and under what conditions. This could create a repeatable process across future disasters.


17.12.2 2. Build a shared analytics capability
By working with government departments, RFA’s data could contribute to dashboards, reports, and analytical tools that show trends across disasters, regions, and intervention types.


17.12.3 3. Strengthen preparedness and recovery planning
Historical response data could help identify recurring vulnerabilities, common service gaps, and high-risk locations. That would make future disaster planning more proactive and better targeted.
crumbs: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance

title: 17  Building a Disaster Data Sharing Partnership at Resilient Futures Alliance
text:
17.13 Operating Constraints
